skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React and Remotion TSX components into MP4 videos through the inference.sh CLI. The skill supports configurable dimensions, frame rate, duration, codec, props, and common Remotion APIs for programmatic motion graphics.
skill.summary: Render configurable Remotion videos from React TSX component code.
skill.author: inference-sh-6

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and URL patterns in SKILL.md. Review confirmed most shell backtick and weak crypto hits are markdown or wording false positives, but the remote installer and infsh rendering workflow are real elevated-risk behaviors that require user warning. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, persistence, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The quick start tells users to run a remote install script with curl piped directly to sh. This is dangerous because script contents can change after review and execute with the user's local privileges. The install note describes checksum verification, but the pipe-to-shell command still requires trust in the remote endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present and semantically executes a remote installer. Confidence is high on the risk, although the surrounding text indicates legitimate installation intent rather than malware.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Sends Render Jobs to Remote Service
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill allows Bash access for infsh commands and documents infsh app run calls that submit TSX component code and props to inference.sh. This is the intended function, but it can expose proprietary code, rendered assets, or prompt-provided data to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and repeated remote run examples clearly show external command and network behavior. The risk is moderate because the behavior is disclosed and central to the skill's purpose.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Flags Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most shell execution findings point to fenced bash, tsx, or python examples in documentation. These snippets demonstrate how to call the remote renderer and are not hidden Ruby backtick execution inside a runtime script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are inside markdown code fences or inline code examples. They are visible documentation, not concealed executable skill code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matching False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not identify use of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or another weak primitive. The relevant security text says SHA-256 checksum verification, and other hits appear to come from ordinary words such as description.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 29 references SHA-256, which is not a weak checksum for this purpose. The other cited lines do not contain cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Documentation and Asset Links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The non-installer URLs point to the inference.sh website, an image asset, Remotion documentation, and inference.sh documentation. No evidence found that these links exfiltrate secrets or contact unrelated infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or asset references that match the skill's stated service. The installer URL is handled separately as a medium finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a curl command piped into sh. Users should prefer manual download, checksum verification, and explicit execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit on the cited line. It is a known risky installation practice even when the provider is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowlisted External CLI Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access to infsh commands. This is required for rendering but means marketplace users rely on the external CLI and service behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowlist is explicit in the skill frontmatter. The risk is bounded to infsh commands but still involves external execution.

content.user_title: Render React Animations as MP4 Videos
content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable video generation without hand-editing every frame. This skill runs Remotion component code through inference.sh to produce configurable MP4 renders.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh Remotion render jobs from documented infsh commands.
Accepts React TSX components that export a default Remotion component.
Supports video width, height, frame rate, duration, codec, and composition ID settings.
Passes props into rendered components for data-driven videos.
Documents common Remotion APIs, including frames, interpolation, springs, sequences, images, audio, and video.
Shows Python SDK usage for batch rendering and streaming progress.
limitations: Requires an installed and authenticated inference.sh CLI or SDK.
Sends render code and input props to the inference.sh service.
Only documents imports from Remotion and React.
Does not provide local Remotion project scaffolding or editing tools.

use_cases.title: Automated Marketing Clips
use_cases.description: Generate consistent product announcement videos from reusable Remotion components and changing copy.

use_cases.title: Data-Driven Status Videos
use_cases.description: Render charts, counters, and progress visuals from props supplied by a workflow or report.

use_cases.title: Developer Video Prototypes
use_cases.description: Test motion ideas as React components before moving them into a larger Remotion project.

prompt_templates.title: Render a Simple Title
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a short text animation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a three second Remotion title animation and render it with remotion-render. Use 1920 by 1080 resolution and 30 FPS.

prompt_templates.title: Render With Custom Props
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for reusable component inputs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Remotion component that accepts title, subtitle, and brand color props. Render one MP4 using these values.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Sequence Animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for timed motion sections.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a four scene Remotion sequence with fade-ins, a spring movement, and a final call to action. Render it as a 1080p MP4.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Render Variants
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for repeatable campaign output.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a reusable Remotion component and render three video variants with different props, durations, and aspect ratios.

output_examples.input: Render a three second welcome title at 1920 by 1080 and 30 FPS.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a Remotion title component, runs an inference.sh render job, and returns the resulting MP4 link.

output_examples.input: Create a square animated counter for a social post.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses a Remotion counter animation, sets a 1080 by 1080 canvas, and reports render progress until the video is ready.

output_examples.input: Render a branded video using title and subtitle props.
output_examples.output: The assistant passes the supplied props into the component and produces a video with matching text and styling.

best_practices: Review generated TSX before sending it to the remote render service.
Use manual installation and checksum verification instead of piping remote scripts to a shell.
Keep proprietary assets, tokens, and private customer data out of component code and props.
anti_patterns: Do not render unreviewed user-supplied TSX that may contain unwanted imports or network references.
Do not include secrets, API keys, or private files in props or component strings.
Do not assume the skill installs or authenticates inference.sh for you.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React and Remotion TSX components into MP4 videos through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it run locally?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow uses the inference.sh CLI or Python SDK to submit remote render jobs.

faq.question: Can I set output dimensions?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show width, height, FPS, duration, codec, composition ID, and props.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The quick start uses a remote installer piped to sh, and render inputs are sent to inference.sh.

faq.question: Should I publish it?
faq.answer: It can be published with a warning about remote execution, external service use, and safer installation steps.
